nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reef slopes and lagoons, where individuals hover above live rock and forage for zooplankton.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with crevices and overhangs

diet.notes: Tends to be a mid-water planktivore in the wild but adapts readily to captive feeding. Generally advised to offer a varied mix of mysis, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and herbivore preparations. High metabolism — small feedings 2–3 times per day are commonly recommended.

goodWith: most peaceful and semi-aggressive reef tankmates
avoidWith: other Meiacanthus species in the same tank
very large or boisterous predators that may stress or harass it

summary: A bright-yellow Fiji fang blenny that is generally considered reef-safe, peaceful with most tankmates, and widely available as a captive-bred species. Carries venomous lower-jaw fangs used in defense.
careNotes:
VENOMOUS FANGS: Meiacanthus oualanensis and its congeners possess two enlarged, grooved canines in the lower jaw connected to venom glands; the venom is delivered via a defensive bite rather than spines, and is reported to feel similar to a bee or wasp sting. Bites to humans are not lethal but are painful — hand-feeding and careless tank maintenance are strongly discouraged. The venom also deters most predators, and several non-venomous mimic species (e.g. Petroscirtes breviceps) imitate Meiacanthus coloration. 

Care-wise this is one of the more forgiving marine blennies: captive-bred specimens from ORA and other breeders are widely available and adapt readily to prepared foods. Best kept singly (or as a confirmed mated pair) per tank — they will not tolerate conspecifics or other Meiacanthus, but are otherwise peaceful toward unrelated tankmates and are generally considered reef-safe with corals, clams, and ornamental shrimp. 

Provide mature live rock with crevices for retreat plus open mid-water swimming space. They tend to hover and dart in the water column rather than sit on the substrate. Has been known to jump if startled or harassed, so a tight-fitting lid is recommended (escape risk noted as moderate). 

Cross-source notes: maximum size is reported as 5" (~12.7 cm) by retail sources (LiveAquaria, AquariumDomain) and 10 cm SL by FishBase / Fishkeeper.co.uk — averaged to ~7–11 cm here. Fishkeeper.co.uk lists 'Western Indian Ocean' as the range, which contradicts FishBase and all other sources; the species is endemic to Fiji (Western Central Pacific). Tank size was rounded up from the commonly-cited 30-gallon (≈114 L) minimum to 120 L per project nice-number convention and the species' active mid-water swimming habit.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity at commercial scale (notably by ORA). Pairs form readily when a male and female are introduced together young, and males will defend small caves where females deposit demersal egg clutches; eggs hatch into pelagic larvae that require small live foods (rotifers, copepod nauplii). Generally considered a moderately difficult captive-bred species — easier than most marine fish but still requires larval-rearing infrastructure.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Canonical LiveAquaria product page for Canary Blenny. Provided care level, tank minimum, water parameters, diet, temperament, venomous-fang warning.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Confirmed tank minimum (30 gal), reef-safe status, semi-aggressive temperament, jump/escape risk, and venomous fangs. Also confirmed water temp upper bound of 80°F.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk
additional.notes: Provided 10 cm adult size figure and 23–27°C temperature range. Note: source incorrectly lists 'Western Indian Ocean' as the range; the species is actually Fiji-endemic (Western Central Pacific) per FishBase.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority. Family Blenniidae, Order Blenniiformes, max length 10 cm SL, distribution Western Central Pacific (Fiji), IUCN Least Concern. Cross-checked secondary as Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; substituted FishBase per playbook fallback rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive-bred at commercial scale (ORA and other producers). Pair-spawning species with demersal eggs in caves, pelagic larvae requiring rotifers and copepod nauplii.
